one_line_takeaway: By injecting the same certified DP noise budget sequentially into orthogonal parameter blocks instead of the whole model at once, and replacing worst-case model-clipping with an empirically-calibrated proximity bound Delta(rho), Block-wise NFT keeps the (epsilon,delta) certified-unlearning guarantee of Koloskova et al.'s NFT while avoiding NFT's severe accuracy collapse (e.g., ViT-Tiny/CIFAR-10 dropping from 88% to under 20%) and matching Retrain's UA/MIA scores on classwise CIFAR-10 deletion at a fraction of retraining's runtime.
key_finding: On CIFAR-10 with ViT-Tiny, standard NFT's test accuracy collapses from 88% to below 20% once unlearning begins and does not recover after 300 further fine-tuning steps (Figure 1), whereas across the tested privacy budgets on MNIST (epsilon=30, 60, 75; delta=0.001) and CIFAR-10 (epsilon=50, 70, 90; delta=0.001) for random 10% deletion, Block-wise NFT (k=2,4,10) produces smoother, more stable unlearning trajectories and stronger post-fine-tuning recovery than Delta(rho)-calibrated NFT, with gains not strongly sensitive to k in this range (Figure 3). On the auxiliary classwise (class-5) CIFAR-10 deletion benchmark (Table 1, baselines reproduced from the official SaLUN repo), Block-wise NFT reaches UA=100.00% (matching Retrain's 100.00%) and MIA=100 (matching Retrain), with RA=96.18% (retrain 100.00%), TA=83.37% (retrain 86.14%), and RTE=0.85 minutes (versus Retrain's 46.37 minutes) -- a lower runtime than every empirical baseline reported with UA=100% (SalUN RTE=2.76, ℓ1-sparse RTE=2.55, AMUN RTE=2.86).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is Block-wise Noisy Fine-Tuning (Block-wise NFT), a modification of the certified-unlearning method 'Noisy Fine-Tuning' (NFT) of Koloskova et al. [19]. Instead of injecting Gaussian noise into all d parameters at once each step, the authors partition the parameter space into k mutually orthogonal blocks (via a layer-wise random orthogonal decomposition, W = sum_i A_i B_i), freeze all but one block, and apply the certified noisy update sequentially to one block at a time, cycling through all k blocks before a final full-model fine-tuning phase. They (1) prove a per-step noise lower bound (Theorem 3.1) showing why standard NFT must inject non-negligible Gaussian noise into every coordinate at every step under the Renyi-DP shifted-divergence proof technique used by [19], explaining the observed accuracy collapse; (2) extend the certification proof of [19] to the block-wise schedule (Theorem 4.2, with a decomposed Wasserstein distance / decomposed shifted Renyi divergence and a Decomposed Shift Reduction Lemma for Gaussians, Lemma 4.6) to show the same (epsilon, delta) unlearning guarantee is retained when per-block budgets sum via composition; and (3) replace NFT's worst-case initial model-clipping radius C0 with a high-probability, empirically calibrated proximity parameter Delta(rho) bounding the distance between the fully-trained model and the retained-data retrained model (Definition 3.4), motivated by a thought experiment (Proposition 3.2) showing worst-case, initialization-agnostic certification creates an inherent utility/speed tension versus retraining.
threat_model: Not an adversarial-attack paper; the relevant 'threat' is privacy leakage about the deleted (forget) data after unlearning. The paper adopts the (epsilon, delta)-unlearning definition of Koloskova et al. [19] (an indistinguishability guarantee between the unlearned model's output distribution and that of a retrained-from-retained-data reference model, analogous to differential privacy) as its formal guarantee, and separately audits empirical leakage using membership inference attacks (MIA) per the SaLUN/ℓ1-sparsity evaluation protocol [17], reporting an MIA score alongside standard unlearning utility metrics. No new attack is proposed; MIA is used only as an auditing tool for the empirical-baseline comparison, and the (epsilon, delta) certificate is the paper's primary formal guarantee.
limitations: The authors explicitly state Theorem 3.1's lower bound is 'proof-technique limited, not an information-theoretic impossibility' -- it only shows the specific Renyi-DP shifted-divergence analysis of [19] requires this much noise, not that no certified method could use less. Delta(rho) is instantiated empirically via a specific coupling (10 runs, high-quantile aggregate) rather than from a theoretical bound, is 'not universal' and must be recalibrated if the deletion regime changes substantially; the paper does not claim this empirical instantiation is optimal. The layer-wise random orthogonal decomposition requires quadratic memory in the decomposed layer dimension (cheaper alternatives -- random permutations, layer-wise grouping -- trade off structure/cost and are only partly explored). The block-wise schedule increases the total number of noisy-update steps (kT instead of T), though the authors note total runtime is dominated by post-unlearning fine-tuning so this cost is small in their experiments. On the classwise CIFAR-10 benchmark, Block-wise NFT's RA and TA are below those of most empirical (uncertified) baselines, i.e. the certified method still trades some utility for its formal guarantee. Proposition 3.2's thought experiment relies on retraining time to match a slightly weakened guarantee being comparable to retraining time for the original guarantee, an assumption the authors say is formalized with caveats only in Appendix C.2 (not reproduced in the portion reviewed).

quantitative_results: Standard NFT on CIFAR-10/ViT-Tiny: test accuracy drops from 88% to below 20% during unlearning and does not recover after 300 fine-tuning steps
Table 1 (CIFAR-10, class-5 deletion): Retrain UA=100.00%, RA=100.00%, TA=86.14%, MIA=100, RTE=46.37 min
Table 1: Block-wise NFT UA=100.00% (+0.00 vs retrain), RA=96.18% (-3.82), TA=83.37% (-2.77), MIA=100, RTE=0.85 min
Figure 3 experiments run at MNIST epsilon=30,60,75 (delta=0.001) and CIFAR-10 epsilon=50,70,90 (delta=0.001), block counts k=2,4,10, results averaged over 5 runs
